title: Message Bus Evolution
description: The evolution of component communication in Android: from callbacks to SharedFlow

references.title: Jose Alcerreca — LiveData with SnackBar, Navigation, and other events (the SingleLiveEvent post)
Roman Elizarov — Shared flows, broadcast channels

lesson:
Why This History Matters

Android has gone through at least six generations of component communication patterns. Legacy codebases still use older approaches, and interview questions often ask you to compare them. Understanding the progression reveals why each successor was invented and what trade-offs it solved.

1. Callbacks / Interfaces (2008+)

The original pattern: define an interface, hold a reference, call back when ready.

// Step 1: Define a contract interface OnUserLoadedListener { fun onUserLoaded(user: User) fun onError(error: Throwable) } // Step 2: Producer holds a reference to the listener class UserRepository { private var listener: OnUserLoadedListener? = null fun setListener(l: OnUserLoadedListener) { listener = l // Danger: this is a strong reference } fun loadUser(id: String) { thread { val user = api.fetchUser(id) listener?.onUserLoaded(user) // Which thread are we on? } } } // Step 3: Activity registers itself class ProfileActivity : AppCompatActivity(), OnUserLoadedListener { override fun onCreate(savedInstanceState: Bundle?) { repository.setListener(this) // Activity leaked if not cleared } override fun onUserLoaded(user: User) { // Might crash: activity could be destroyed by now nameTextView.text = user.name } override fun onDestroy() { super.onDestroy() repository.setListener(null) // Must remember to do this! } }
Problems with callbacks:
1. Reference leaks — producer holds the consumer; if you forget to null it, the Activity leaks
2. No lifecycle awareness — callback fires on a destroyed Activity = crash
3. Threading is your problem — callback fires on whatever thread the producer used
4. Tight coupling — producer must know the listener interface; 1:1 only
5. Callback hell — nested callbacks for sequential async operations become unreadable

2. LocalBroadcastManager (2011–2018, deprecated)

Part of the Support Library. Provided an in-process event bus using Intent and BroadcastReceiver.

// Sending val intent = Intent("com.example.USER_LOADED") intent.putExtra("user_name", user.name) LocalBroadcastManager.getInstance(context).sendBroadcast(intent) // Receiving val receiver = object : BroadcastReceiver() { override fun onReceive(context: Context, intent: Intent) { val name = intent.getStringExtra("user_name") } } LocalBroadcastManager.getInstance(context) .registerReceiver(receiver, IntentFilter("com.example.USER_LOADED")) // Must unregister in onDestroy! LocalBroadcastManager.getInstance(context).unregisterReceiver(receiver)
Why deprecated: Google's deprecation notice says it was "an anti-pattern" because it combined the heavyweight Intent/BroadcastReceiver API (designed for cross-process IPC) with in-process use. It offered no lifecycle awareness, required manual unregister, used string-based actions (no type safety), and could only carry Bundle-serializable data. LiveData and reactive streams were strictly better.

3. Otto / EventBus (greenrobot) (2012–2017)

Third-party publish-subscribe libraries that decoupled producers from consumers. EventBus by greenrobot became the dominant choice.

// Define an event class (any POJO) data class UserLoadedEvent(val user: User) // Post from anywhere EventBus.getDefault().post(UserLoadedEvent(user)) // Subscribe in Activity/Fragment class ProfileActivity : AppCompatActivity() { override fun onStart() { super.onStart() EventBus.getDefault().register(this) } override fun onStop() { super.onStop() EventBus.getDefault().unregister(this) } @Subscribe(threadMode = ThreadMode.MAIN) fun onUserLoaded(event: UserLoadedEvent) { nameTextView.text = event.user.name } }

ThreadMode options in greenrobot EventBus:

ThreadModeBehavior
POSTINGSame thread as poster (default)
MAINAlways on Android main thread
MAIN_ORDEREDMain thread, enqueued (non-blocking post)
BACKGROUNDBackground thread (if posted from main, new thread; if posted from BG, same thread)
ASYNCAlways a separate thread from a pool
Why EventBus fell out of favor:
1. Debugging nightmare — Ctrl+F for post(UserLoadedEvent) is the only way to find who sends it. No call hierarchy, no IDE navigation from producer to consumer
2. No compile-time safety — misspell the event class? No subscriber found, silent failure
3. Implicit coupling — everything talks to everything via a global bus; dependency graph becomes invisible
4. Sticky events leak — sticky events persist until manually removed, causing stale data bugs
5. Still no lifecycle awareness — must manually register/unregister; miss onStop = crash

4. RxJava Subjects as Event Bus (~2015–2019)

As RxJava gained popularity, teams used Subject types as in-process event buses. This gave them the reactive operator chain they already knew.

// Singleton event bus using RxJava object RxBus { private val bus = PublishSubject.create<Any>() fun post(event: Any) = bus.onNext(event) // Callers filter by type inline fun <reified T> observe(): Observable<T> = bus.ofType(T::class.java) } // In Activity — must dispose to avoid leaks class ProfileActivity : AppCompatActivity() { private val disposables = CompositeDisposable() override fun onCreate(savedInstanceState: Bundle?) { super.onCreate(savedInstanceState) disposables.add( RxBus.observe<UserLoadedEvent>() .observeOn(AndroidSchedulers.mainThread()) .subscribe { event -> nameTextView.text = event.user.name } ) } override fun onDestroy() { disposables.clear() // Forget this = memory leak super.onDestroy() } }

Subject types in RxJava:

SubjectBehaviorUse Case
PublishSubjectOnly emits items after subscriptionEvents (clicks, navigation)
BehaviorSubjectReplays last emitted item to new subscribersState (current user, settings)
ReplaySubjectReplays all items to new subscribersChat history, logs
AsyncSubjectOnly emits the last item, on completionSingle async result
Trade-offs of RxJava as event bus:
+ Powerful operators (debounce, flatMap, retry, combine)
+ Type-safe (generics, not Any-based reflection)
+ Thread control with subscribeOn / observeOn
- Steep learning curve; error handling is complex (onError terminates the stream)
- CompositeDisposable is manual lifecycle management
- RxJava 2+ adds a 2.5 MB dependency; method count matters on Android
- No lifecycle awareness out of the box

5. LiveData (2017+)

Google's first lifecycle-aware observable. Built into Architecture Components.

// ViewModel exposes LiveData class ProfileViewModel : ViewModel() { private val _user = MutableLiveData<User>() val user: LiveData<User> = _user fun loadUser(id: String) { viewModelScope.launch { _user.value = repository.fetchUser(id) } } } // Activity observes — automatically stops when lifecycle is DESTROYED class ProfileActivity : AppCompatActivity() { override fun onCreate(savedInstanceState: Bundle?) { super.onCreate(savedInstanceState) viewModel.user.observe(this) { user -> nameTextView.text = user.name } } // No onDestroy cleanup needed! LiveData respects the lifecycle. }
The Event Wrapper Hack:
LiveData replays the last value to new observers. This is great for state ("show the current user") but terrible for events ("show a Snackbar"). After rotation, the Snackbar shows again because LiveData re-delivers the last value.

The community invented SingleLiveEvent and the Event wrapper class to work around this:
// The famous Event wrapper (Jose Alcerreca, Google) open class Event<out T>(private val content: T) { private var hasBeenHandled = false fun getContentIfNotHandled(): T? { return if (hasBeenHandled) null else { hasBeenHandled = true content } } } // Usage in ViewModel private val _showSnackbar = MutableLiveData<Event<String>>() val showSnackbar: LiveData<Event<String>> = _showSnackbar fun onSaveClicked() { save() _showSnackbar.value = Event("Saved successfully") } // Usage in Activity — notice the unwrapping boilerplate viewModel.showSnackbar.observe(this) { event -> event.getContentIfNotHandled()?.let { message -> Snackbar.make(root, message, Snackbar.LENGTH_SHORT).show() } }

This works but is widely considered a hack. It also breaks if there are multiple observers (only the first one gets the event).

6. SharedFlow & StateFlow (2020+) — The Modern Answer

Kotlin coroutines introduced SharedFlow and StateFlow, which solve the state-vs-event problem cleanly.

// STATE: Use StateFlow (replays last value, like BehaviorSubject) class ProfileViewModel : ViewModel() { private val _user = MutableStateFlow<User?>(null) val user: StateFlow<User?> = _user.asStateFlow() fun loadUser(id: String) { viewModelScope.launch { _user.value = repository.fetchUser(id) } } } // EVENTS: Use SharedFlow with replay=0 (like PublishSubject) class ProfileViewModel : ViewModel() { private val _events = MutableSharedFlow<UiEvent>() val events: SharedFlow<UiEvent> = _events.asSharedFlow() fun onSaveClicked() { viewModelScope.launch { save() _events.emit(UiEvent.ShowSnackbar("Saved!")) // No Event wrapper needed! replay=0 means no re-delivery } } } sealed class UiEvent { data class ShowSnackbar(val message: String) : UiEvent() data class Navigate(val route: String) : UiEvent() } // Collecting in Activity/Fragment — lifecycle-aware with repeatOnLifecycle lifecycleScope.launch { repeatOnLifecycle(Lifecycle.State.STARTED) { launch { viewModel.user.collect { user -> user?.let { nameTextView.text = it.name } } } launch { viewModel.events.collect { event -> when (event) { is UiEvent.ShowSnackbar -> Snackbar.make(root, event.message, LENGTH_SHORT).show() is UiEvent.Navigate -> navController.navigate(event.route) } } } } }
Why SharedFlow/StateFlow is the modern standard:
- StateFlow for state: always has a value, replays the latest to new collectors, conflates rapid updates
- SharedFlow(replay=0) for events: no re-delivery on rotation, no Event wrapper hack
- Lifecycle-safe with repeatOnLifecycle (auto-cancels collection when lifecycle drops below threshold)
- Coroutine scope handles cancellation automatically
- Full operator support (map, filter, combine, debounce, etc.)

Full Comparison Table

AspectCallbacksLocalBroadcastEventBusRxJava SubjectLiveDataSharedFlow/StateFlow
CouplingTight (interface ref)Loose (string action)Loose (event class)Loose (type filter)Loose (observe)Loose (collect)
Type SafetyYesNo (Intent extras)Partial (reflection)Yes (generics)YesYes
Lifecycle AwareNoNoNoNoYesYes (with repeatOnLifecycle)
ThreadingManual (runOnUiThread)Main thread onlyThreadMode annotationobserveOn/subscribeOnMain thread autoDispatcher / flowOn
CancellationManual null refunregisterReceiver()unregister()CompositeDisposableAuto (lifecycle)Auto (scope cancel)
Events vs StateEvents onlyEvents onlyBoth (sticky = state)Both (Subject type)State (events = hack)Both (SharedFlow/StateFlow)
TestabilityEasy (mock interface)Hard (needs Context)Medium (post + verify)Good (TestObserver)Good (observeForever)Best (Turbine library)
DebuggingGood (stack trace)Poor (string matching)Poor (no call graph)Medium (operator chain)GoodGood (suspend stack)
Status (2025)Still used for simple UI callbacksDeprecatedLegacy, avoid in new codeBeing replaced by FlowStable, losing ground to FlowRecommended standard

Threading Comparison

How each approach handles "run work on background, deliver result on main":

// Callback — threading is completely manual thread { val user = api.fetchUser(id) runOnUiThread { listener?.onUserLoaded(user) } } // EventBus — annotation-based @Subscribe(threadMode = ThreadMode.MAIN) fun onEvent(event: UserLoadedEvent) { ... } // RxJava — operator-based repository.fetchUser(id) .subscribeOn(Schedulers.io()) .observeOn(AndroidSchedulers.mainThread()) .subscribe { user -> ... } // LiveData — always delivers on main, work done in coroutine viewModelScope.launch { val user = withContext(Dispatchers.IO) { api.fetchUser(id) } _user.value = user // Must be on main thread } // StateFlow — dispatcher controls where work runs, flowOn for upstream flow { emit(api.fetchUser(id)) } .flowOn(Dispatchers.IO) .collect { user -> updateUi(user) } // Collector's dispatcher

Cancellation Comparison

How each approach prevents work from continuing after the consumer is gone:

Callback: onDestroy() { repository.setListener(null) } // Forget = leak ↳ Must manually null every reference LocalBroadcastManager: onDestroy() { lbm.unregisterReceiver(receiver) } // Forget = leak ↳ Must manually unregister EventBus: onStop() { EventBus.getDefault().unregister(this) } // Forget = crash ↳ Must manually unregister, usually in onStop RxJava: onDestroy() { disposables.clear() } // Forget = leak + possible crash ↳ CompositeDisposable tracks all subscriptions ↳ RxLifecycle / AutoDispose libraries tried to automate this LiveData: // Nothing! observe(lifecycleOwner, ...) auto-removes on DESTROYED ↳ First approach with built-in lifecycle cancellation SharedFlow / StateFlow: // Nothing! repeatOnLifecycle auto-cancels collection // viewModelScope auto-cancels when ViewModel is cleared ↳ Structured concurrency = automatic cancellation hierarchy
The trend is clear: each generation reduced the amount of manual cleanup code. Callbacks required nulling every reference. EventBus required register/unregister pairs. RxJava required CompositeDisposable. LiveData and Flow finally made cancellation automatic through lifecycle awareness and structured concurrency.

questions.title: Why LocalBroadcastManager Was Deprecated
options: It had a critical security vulnerability that allowed cross-app data leaks
It used the heavyweight Intent/BroadcastReceiver IPC pattern for in-process communication, lacked lifecycle awareness, had no type safety, and LiveData/reactive streams were strictly better
It was too slow due to serialization overhead
It only worked on API 21+ and Google wanted backwards compatibility
questions.explanation: Google's own deprecation notice called it "an anti-pattern." LocalBroadcastManager borrowed the Intent and BroadcastReceiver API — designed for cross-process IPC — and used it within a single process. This meant no type safety (string-based actions, Bundle extras), no lifecycle awareness (manual unregister required), and all the ceremony of intents without any of the cross-process benefits. LiveData and reactive streams solved the same problem with type safety, lifecycle awareness, and cleaner APIs.
hints: Think about what Intent and BroadcastReceiver were originally designed for
Consider type safety and lifecycle management

questions.title: EventBus ThreadMode
questions.description: What happens when this code runs? Assume postEvent() is called from a background thread.
options: The subscriber runs on the main thread and the RecyclerView updates correctly
The subscriber runs on the SAME background thread that posted, causing a CalledFromWrongThreadException crash when touching the RecyclerView
EventBus automatically switches to the main thread for UI operations
The event is silently dropped because of thread mismatch
questions.explanation: ThreadMode.POSTING (the default) means the subscriber method runs on the same thread as the poster. Since the event was posted from a background thread, the subscriber also runs on that background thread. Calling notifyDataSetChanged() from a background thread throws CalledFromWrongThreadException because only the main thread can touch Views. The fix is to use ThreadMode.MAIN instead.
hints: POSTING means same thread as the poster
What happens when you touch Android Views from a background thread?

questions.title: The Event Wrapper Problem
questions.description: Why does LiveData need the Event wrapper class for one-time events like showing a Snackbar?
options: LiveData cannot emit null values, so the wrapper provides a nullable alternative
LiveData replays the last emitted value to new/returning observers, so after a screen rotation the Snackbar would show again without the wrapper
LiveData does not support sealed classes, so events must be wrapped
The Event wrapper provides type erasure for generic LiveData types
questions.explanation: LiveData is designed for state — it always holds the latest value and re-delivers it when a new observer subscribes or when the lifecycle returns to STARTED (e.g., after rotation). This is perfect for "show the current user name" but breaks for one-time events like Snackbar messages or navigation commands. The Event wrapper tracks whether the content has been consumed with a hasBeenHandled flag, but it's widely considered a hack. SharedFlow with replay = 0 solves this without any wrapper.
hints: Think about what happens to a LiveData observer after a configuration change
State vs events: state should replay, events should not

questions.title: SharedFlow vs StateFlow for Events
questions.description: What is the critical difference in behavior between these two ViewModel implementations for one-time navigation events?
options: Both work the same way for navigation events
A replays the navigation after rotation (navigates again). B does not replay (replay=0 default), so navigation happens only once. B is correct for events.
B drops events if no collector is active. A is safer because it holds the value.
A is more efficient because StateFlow conflates values
questions.explanation: StateFlow always replays its current value to new collectors, just like LiveData. After a screen rotation, the collector re-subscribes, receives "detail/$id" again, and navigates a second time. SharedFlow with the default replay = 0 does not replay — events are delivered only to currently active collectors. This makes SharedFlow the correct choice for one-time events like navigation, Snackbar, and dialog triggers. Note: if you need to guarantee delivery even when no collector is active, consider using a Channel instead.
hints: StateFlow always has a current value and replays it
What is the default replay count for MutableSharedFlow()?

questions.title: RxJava CompositeDisposable
questions.description: What happens if you forget to call disposables.clear() in onDestroy() when using an RxJava-based event bus?
options: Nothing — RxJava automatically handles lifecycle cleanup

The Activity is leaked because the subscription holds a reference chain: RxBus → Observable → Observer → lambda capturing the Activity (via nameTextView). Events dispatched after destroy may also crash.
A compile-time error prevents the code from building without onDestroy
Only the Observable leaks, the Activity is garbage collected normally
questions.explanation: The RxJava subscription creates a reference chain: the global RxBus Subject holds the Observable, which holds the Observer (subscriber), which captures the lambda, which references nameTextView, which holds a reference to the Activity. Since the RxBus Subject lives as a singleton, the Activity can never be garbage collected. Additionally, if a UserEvent is posted after the Activity is destroyed, the subscriber tries to update nameTextView on a destroyed Activity, potentially causing a crash. This is exactly why lifecycle-aware solutions like LiveData and repeatOnLifecycle with Flow were created.
hints: Think about the reference chain from the singleton bus to the Activity
What happens when an event fires after the Activity is destroyed?

questions.title: Debugging EventBus vs Direct Calls
questions.description: A teammate is tracking down a bug where a CartUpdatedEvent is causing the wrong UI update. They're using greenrobot EventBus. What makes this harder to debug than a direct method call?
options: EventBus encrypts events in transit, making them unreadable in the debugger

There is no direct call graph from poster to subscriber — you must search the entire codebase for @Subscribe methods accepting CartUpdatedEvent, and any class could be posting it. The IDE cannot show 'Find Usages' from post to subscriber.
EventBus runs subscribers in random order, making the bug non-reproducible
EventBus events are asynchronous by default and cannot be stepped through
questions.explanation: With a direct method call like cartManager.updateCart(), you can Ctrl+click to the implementation, see all callers via "Find Usages," and trace the full call stack. With EventBus, the producer calls EventBus.post(CartUpdatedEvent(...)) and the consumer has @Subscribe fun onCartUpdated(event: CartUpdatedEvent) — there is no compile-time link between them. You must manually search the codebase for all post(CartUpdatedEvent calls and all @Subscribe methods with that parameter type. As the codebase grows, this becomes a major maintenance burden and is one of the primary reasons EventBus fell out of favor.
hints: Think about what happens when you try to Ctrl+click from a post() call to find who handles it
How many classes could potentially subscribe to the same event?

questions.title: Cancellation Across Generations
questions.description: A developer is migrating from EventBus to SharedFlow. Which statement about the cancellation behavior is correct?
options: Both require manual cleanup in onStop/onDestroy
The EventBus version is safer because it uses symmetric register/unregister

The SharedFlow version automatically cancels collection when the lifecycle drops below STARTED and restarts when it returns — no manual cleanup. The EventBus version crashes if you forget unregister().
The SharedFlow version leaks because there is no explicit cancel() call
questions.explanation: repeatOnLifecycle(Lifecycle.State.STARTED) automatically cancels the collecting coroutine when the lifecycle drops below STARTED (e.g., fragment goes to background) and restarts it when the lifecycle returns to STARTED (fragment comes back to foreground). There is no manual cleanup code needed. The EventBus version relies on the developer correctly pairing register() in onStart with unregister() in onStop. Forgetting unregister() means the fragment receives events while in a stopped state, which can cause crashes when accessing views that have been destroyed.
hints: What does repeatOnLifecycle actually do under the hood?
Consider what happens if you forget the manual cleanup in each version

questions.title: PublishSubject vs BehaviorSubject
questions.description: You have a global theme manager that should immediately tell new subscribers the current theme, and a notification system that should only deliver notifications going forward. Which RxJava Subject types would you use?
options: PublishSubject for both — subscribers should only get future events
BehaviorSubject for the theme manager (replays last value to new subscribers), PublishSubject for notifications (only future events)
ReplaySubject for the theme manager, AsyncSubject for notifications
BehaviorSubject for both — new subscribers always need the latest state
questions.explanation: BehaviorSubject replays the most recent value to every new subscriber, making it perfect for state like the current theme — a new screen immediately knows whether to render light or dark. PublishSubject only emits items that arrive after subscription, making it correct for events like notifications — you don't want a new subscriber to receive an old notification they already missed. This maps directly to the modern Kotlin equivalents: StateFlow (always has a value, replays latest) = BehaviorSubject, SharedFlow(replay=0) (no replay) = PublishSubject.
hints: Does a new screen need to know the current theme immediately, or only future theme changes?
Should a newly opened screen show old notifications?
